content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 9, 1990


Iris P. Yang
McDonough, Holland and Allen
Attorneys at Law
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 950
Sacramento, CA  95814

                                        Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                             Our File No. A-90-536

Dear Ms. Yang:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice regard-
ing the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act")¹ and confirms the telephone advice provided to you
on September 18, 1990.

                            QUESTION

     Under the provisions of the Act, may a county supervisor
represent the interests of a client before the county planning
commission and the county board of supervisors?

                            CONCLUSION

     Under the provisions of the Act, a county supervisor may
represent the interests of a client before the county planning
commission and before the county board of supervisors when the
client is a corporation wholly owned by the supervisor's minor
children.

                            FACTS

     Sierra County Supervisor Craig McHenry has established an
irrevocable family trust, the sole beneficiaries of which are his
three minor children.  The trust was funded by Supervisor McHenry


_________________________
     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento CA 95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

File No. A-90-536
Page 2

and his spouse.² Supervisor McHenry has retained discretion as to
the distribution of the trust among his three minor children. A
third party trustee has been appointed to represent the interests
of the trust.

The family trust owns all of the stock of a corporation which
owns certain real property within Sierra County. The corporation
has applied for a conditional use permit to allow for the storage
and repair of logging equipment on its property. If the
conditional use permit is granted, the corporation would lease the
property to Mr. Edward Hood, who would store and repair his
company's trucks on the property. Mr. Hood leased the
property from the corporation in the past when the applicant was
originally granted a similar use permit.

Supervisor McHenry is an attorney. He has represented and
continues to represent Mr. Hood who is a source of income to him.
In addition to his logging business, Mr. Hood has a garbage hauling business and has a hauling contract with Sierra County.
Supervisor McHenry has never voted on the hauling contract or its
renewal when it was before the board of supervisors. Supervisor
McHenry also understands that he may not vote as a member of the
board of supervisors on the conditional use permit application.

As Supervisor McHenry's legal counsel, you seek our advice to
determine whether Supervisor McHenry may represent the applicant
for the use permit before the Sierra County Planning Commission
and the Sierra County Board of Supervisors pursuant to Regulation
18700.1(b) (copy enclosed). As stated above, the applicant is a
corporation which is wholly owned by the family trust, the sole
beneficiaries of which are Supervisor McHenry's minor children.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his or
participate in using or attempting to influence a governmental decision in
which the official knows or has reason to know he or she has a
financial interest. A member of a county board of supervisors is
a public official. (Section 82048.)

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on:

     (a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

_____________________________

² These facts include information you provided to me during our
telephone conversation of September 7, 1990.

File No. A-90-536
Page 3

     (b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dol-
lars ($250) or more in value provided to, received
by or promised to the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.

     (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

     (e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

     For purposes of this section, indirect invest-
ment or interest means any investment or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
official, by an agent on behalf of a public of-
ficial, or by a business entity or trust in which
the official, the official's agents, spouse, and
dependent children own directly, indirectly, or
beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater.

Section 87103.

     Your facts indicate that Supervisor McHenry has established
an irrevocable family trust for the benefit of his three minor
children. This trust holds the stock of a corporation which owns
certain real property within Sierra County. Supervisor McHenry
has not received any income from the trust or from the corporation
in the twelve months preceding the decision. However, pursuant to
the provisions of Section 87103, Supervisor McHenry has an
indirect investment or interest in the trust established for the
benefit of his dependent children. For purposes of our analysis
we assume that Supervisor McHenry has a direct or indirect invest-
ment worth in excess of $1,000 in the corporation. Similarly, for
purposes of our analysis, we assume that Supervisor McHenry has a
direct or indirect interest worth more than $1,000 in the real
property owned by the corporation. Thus, Supervisor McHenry may
not attempt to use his official position to influence a decision
which will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the
corporation in which he has an indirect investment or interest.

File No. A-90-536
Page 4

     Prohibited attempts to influence a decision include contact-
ing any member, officer, employee of the official's agency, or any
agency under the appointive or budgetary control of the official's
agency.  Thus, if Supervisor McHenry's client would be foreseeably
and materially affected by the pending decision, the supervisor
may not represent the client before the county planning commission
which is subject to the appointive and budgetary control of the
county board of supervisors. Additionally, Supervisor McHenry may
not represent his client before his own agency, the board of
supervisors.

     Furthermore, it is foreseeable that Mr. Hood, who has been a
source of income to Supervisor McHenry in excess of $250 in the 12
months preceding the decision, will be materially affected by the
decision. This is so because it is foreseeable that, if the
permit is granted, Mr. Hood will lease the property from the
corporation. Thus, Supervisor McHenry may not participate in the
decision.

     You correctly refer our attention to Regulation 18700.1 which
carves out exceptions to the general rule. This regulation states
in pertinent part as follows:

          (a) With regard to a governmental decision
     which is within or before an official's agency or
     an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
     control of his or her agency, the official is at-
     tempting to use his or her official position to
     influence the decision if, for the purpose of
     influencing the decision, the official contacts,
     appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence,
     any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
     agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not
     limited to, appearances or contacts by the official
     on behalf of a business entity, client, or
     customer.

          (b) Notwithstanding subsection (a) an of-
     ficial is not attempting to use his or her official
     position to influence a governmental decision of an
     agency covered by subsection (a) if the official:

               (1) Appears in the same manner as any
          other member of the general public before an
          agency in the course of its prescribed
          governmental function solely to represent
          himself or herself on a matter which is
          related to his or her personal interests. An
          official's "personal interests" include, but
          are not limited to:

               (A) An interest in real property
          which is wholly owned by the official or
          members of his or her immediate family.

File No. A-90-536
Page 5

(B) A business entity wholly owned
by the official or members of his or her
immediate family.

Regulation 18700.1(a) and (b).

Pursuant to subdivision (b) of Regulation 18700.1, the
general rule does not apply when a public official appears before
his or her agency solely to represent his or her personal
interests, including interests in real property or a business
wholly owned by the official or members of his or her immediate
family.³ Although Supervisor McHenry has divested himself of all
interest in the trust, his dependent children are the sole
beneficiaries of the trust and hold an equitable interest in the
corporation and the real property held by the trust. (Reagh v.
Fahey (1970) 89 Cal. Rptr. 425, 10 Cal. App. 3d 1082.) 
Thus, Supervisor McHenry may appear before the county planning
commission and the board of supervisors to represent the interests
of the corporation in its application for a conditional use
permit. We suggest that Supervisor McHenry clarify that he is not
appearing in his capacity as a county supervisor. The fact that
it is foreseeable that Edward Hood, who is a client and source
of income to Supervisor McHenry, will lease the land if the
conditional use permit is granted does not alter our conclusion.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Blanca M. Breeze
    Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BMB:plh

Enclosure

___________________________
³ "Immediate family" means the spouse and dependent children.
(Section 82029.)

QUESTION Under the provisions of the Act, may a county supervisor represent the interests of a client before the county planning commission and the county board of supervisors? CONCLUSION Under the provisions of the Act, a county supervisor may represent the interests of a client before the county planning commission and before the county board of supervisors when the client is a corporation wholly owned by the supervisor's minor children.
